title: Listen and Choose a Response — Academic Situations

questions.stem: I'm trying to register for Psychology 201, but the system says it's full.

options.A: You can join the waitlist and check for openings during the first week.
options.B: Psychology courses are very popular this semester.

questions.explanation: The speaker is reporting a problem with course registration, so offering the waitlist as a solution is the most helpful response.

questions.stem: We're ordering takeout for the study group tonight. Do you want anything?

options.A: Yes, I'll take a chicken wrap and a soda. I'll pay you back.
options.B: Studying in groups can be very effective.
options.C: I usually cook my own dinner.

questions.explanation: The speaker is offering to include you in a food order, so specifying what you'd like and offering to pay is the natural response.

questions.context: Seeking permission to record a lecture
questions.stem: Professor, would it be alright if I recorded today's lecture? I'm an auditory learner.

options.A: As long as it's for personal use only, that's fine with me.
options.B: Lectures are already posted online every week.

questions.explanation: The speaker is politely asking for permission to record, so granting it with reasonable conditions is appropriate.

questions.stem: I'm frustrated because my group members haven't done their part of the project.

options.A: I understand. Maybe you can talk to the professor about reassigning groups?
options.C: I did my part on time.

questions.explanation: The speaker is expressing frustration about unequal group participation, so sympathizing and suggesting a constructive action is supportive.

questions.stem: I'm considering the exchange program in Japan. Do you know anyone who's done it?

options.A: Yes, my roommate went last year and loved it. I can connect you two.
options.B: Study abroad programs require a minimum GPA.
options.C: Japan is very expensive for students.

questions.explanation: The speaker is asking for firsthand experience about a program, so sharing a relevant connection is directly helpful.

questions.stem: Thanks for letting me borrow your notes. Here they are back — I made some extra annotations too.

options.A: Oh nice, I'll take a look at those annotations later. Thanks!
options.B: I hope my handwriting wasn't too messy.
options.C: Notes are important for exam review.

questions.explanation: The speaker is returning borrowed notes with added value, so acknowledging the gesture and showing appreciation is polite.

questions.stem: Do I need to complete an internship to graduate, or are there other options?

options.A: You can choose between an internship or a capstone research project.
options.C: Graduation requirements are listed in the catalog.

questions.explanation: The speaker is asking about specific graduation requirements, so providing the alternatives available directly answers their question.

questions.stem: I just signed up for Dr. Martinez's class. I've heard she's an amazing lecturer!

options.A: She really is! Her classes are challenging but very rewarding.
options.B: Dr. Martinez teaches several different courses.
options.C: You should buy the textbook before the first class.

questions.explanation: The speaker is expressing excitement about a professor, so sharing in their enthusiasm and providing validation is the natural response.

We're ordering takeout for the study group tonight. Do you want anything?
Professor, would it be alright if I recorded today's lecture? I'm an auditory learner.

I just signed up for Dr. Martinez's class. I've heard she's an amazing lecturer!
